subUnits.title: 1.1 Definition and Terminology of Earthquakes
notes: Earthquake:
• A sudden shaking or trembling of the Earth's crust caused by the release of accumulated energy
• Energy is released in the form of seismic waves that travel through the Earth
• Measured using seismographs and quantified on Richter or Moment Magnitude scales
Key Terms Associated with Earthquakes:
1. Focus/Hypocenter:
• The point within the Earth where the earthquake rupture starts
• Location where seismic energy is first released
• Can be shallow (0-70 km), intermediate (70-300 km), or deep (300-700 km)
2. Epicenter:
• The point on the Earth's surface directly above the focus
• Usually experiences the strongest shaking
• Located using triangulation from at least three seismograph stations
3. Seismic Waves:
• Energy waves generated by earthquakes that travel through Earth
• Types:
a) Body Waves (travel through Earth's interior):
- P-waves (Primary/Pressure waves): Fastest, compressional, travel through solids and liquids
- S-waves (Secondary/Shear waves): Slower, transverse, travel only through solids
b) Surface Waves (travel along Earth's surface):
- Love waves: Horizontal shearing motion, cause most damage
- Rayleigh waves: Rolling motion like ocean waves, cause ground to rise and fall
4. Fault:
• A fracture in Earth's crust along which movement has occurred
• Types: Normal, Reverse, Strike-slip, Transform
5. Tremor:
• A minor earthquake or shaking of the ground
• Often precedes or follows major earthquakes (foreshocks/aftershocks)
6. Magnitude:
• Measure of the energy released at the earthquake source
• Richter Scale: Logarithmic scale (each whole number = 10x amplitude, ~32x energy)
• Moment Magnitude Scale (Mw): More accurate for large earthquakes
7. Intensity:
• Measure of shaking and damage at a specific location
• Modified Mercalli Intensity Scale: I (not felt) to XII (total destruction)
8. Aftershocks:
• Smaller earthquakes that follow the main shock
• Can continue for days, weeks, or months
• Can cause additional damage to weakened structures
9. Foreshocks:
• Smaller earthquakes that precede the main shock
• Not always present; difficult to identify until after main shock

quiz.q: The point within the Earth where an earthquake rupture starts is called the:
quiz.explanation: The focus (or hypocenter) is the actual location within Earth where seismic energy is first released.

quiz.q: The point on Earth's surface directly above the focus is the:
quiz.explanation: The epicenter is the surface projection of the focus and typically experiences the strongest shaking.

quiz.q: Which seismic waves travel fastest and can move through both solids and liquids?
quiz.explanation: P-waves (Primary/Pressure waves) are compressional waves that travel fastest and through all materials.

quiz.q: S-waves cannot travel through:
quiz.explanation: S-waves (Secondary/Shear waves) are transverse waves that cannot propagate through liquids, which helps scientists study Earth's liquid outer core.

quiz.q: The Richter Scale measures earthquake:
quiz.explanation: The Richter Scale is a logarithmic measure of the amplitude of seismic waves, representing energy released at the source (magnitude).

quiz.q: The Modified Mercalli Intensity Scale measures:
opts: Shaking and damage at specific locations
quiz.explanation: Intensity scales like Mercalli describe observed effects and damage at specific locations, varying with distance from epicenter.

quiz.q: Surface waves that cause horizontal shearing motion are called:
quiz.explanation: Love waves produce horizontal shearing motion perpendicular to wave direction, causing significant structural damage.

quiz.q: Aftershocks are:
opts: Earthquakes that precede the main shock
Smaller earthquakes that follow the main shock
Waves that travel on the surface
quiz.explanation: Aftershocks are smaller earthquakes that occur after the main shock as the crust adjusts to new stress conditions.

quiz.q: The Pacific Ring of Fire is significant because it:
opts: Contains most of Earth's volcanoes and earthquakes
Is the safest region for earthquakes
quiz.explanation: The Pacific Ring of Fire is a major area where many earthquakes and volcanic eruptions occur due to plate boundaries.

quiz.q: Triangulation is used to locate an earthquake's:
quiz.explanation: By measuring the time difference between P and S waves at three or more stations, scientists can triangulate the epicenter location.

subUnits.title: 1.2 Causes, Regions and Effects of Earthquakes
notes: Causes of Earthquakes:
1. Tectonic Plate Movements (Most Common):
• Plate Tectonics Theory: Earth's lithosphere is divided into plates that move
• Plate Boundaries:
a) Convergent (Collision): Plates move together → subduction or mountain building
- Example: Himalayas (India-Eurasia collision), Andes (Nazca-South America)
b) Divergent (Spreading): Plates move apart → mid-ocean ridges, rift valleys
- Example: Mid-Atlantic Ridge, East African Rift
c) Transform (Sliding): Plates slide past each other → strike-slip faults
- Example: San Andreas Fault (California)
• Stress builds up along faults until rock strength is exceeded → sudden slip → earthquake
2. Volcanic Activity:
• Magma movement creates pressure and fractures rock
• Often produces swarms of small earthquakes before/during eruptions
• Example: Mount St. Helens (USA), Mount Cameroon
3. Human Activities (Induced Seismicity):
• Reservoir-induced: Large dams adding weight to crust (e.g., Koyna Dam, India)
• Mining: Removal of material causing subsidence
• Fluid injection: Wastewater disposal, fracking altering pore pressure
• Nuclear testing: Underground explosions generating seismic waves
4. Other Natural Causes:
• Landslides and rockfalls
• Cave collapses
• Meteorite impacts (rare)
Major Earthquake Regions of the World:
1. Circum-Pacific Belt (Pacific Ring of Fire):
• ~80% of world's largest earthquakes
• Boundaries: West coasts of Americas, East Asia, Pacific islands
• Countries: Japan, Chile, California, Indonesia, Philippines
2. Mediterranean-Asiatic Belt (Alpine-Himalayan Belt):
• ~15% of world's earthquakes
• Boundaries: Mediterranean, Middle East, Himalayas, Southeast Asia
• Countries: Italy, Turkey, Iran, India, China
3. Mid-Ocean Ridges:
• Divergent boundaries with frequent but usually small earthquakes
• Example: Mid-Atlantic Ridge
4. Intraplate Regions:
• Earthquakes within plate interiors (less frequent but can be severe)
• Examples: New Madrid (USA, 1811-12), Gujarat (India, 2001)
Effects of Earthquakes:
1. Primary Effects (Direct results of ground shaking):
• Ground rupture and displacement along faults
• Building and infrastructure collapse
• Landslides and rockfalls on slopes
• Soil liquefaction: Saturated soil loses strength, behaves like liquid
2. Secondary Effects (Indirect consequences):
• Tsunamis: Ocean earthquakes displacing water → massive waves
- Example: 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami (230,000+ deaths)
• Fires: Broken gas lines, electrical shorts ignite fires
- Example: 1906 San Francisco earthquake fires
• Floods: Dam failures, landslide-dammed rivers
• Disease outbreaks: Contaminated water, poor sanitation post-disaster
3. Human and Economic Impacts:
• Loss of life and injury
• Homelessness and displacement
• Economic losses: Infrastructure repair, business disruption
• Psychological trauma and social disruption
4. Environmental Impacts:
• Changes to landscape: Uplift, subsidence, new faults
• Alteration of groundwater flow and quality
• Damage to ecosystems and habitats
Nigerian Context:
• Nigeria is generally low seismic risk but not earthquake-free
• Minor earthquakes recorded in:
- Abeokuta (1933, 1980s)
- Lagos (occasional minor tremors)
- Northern Nigeria (associated with African-Eurasian plate interaction)
• Preparedness important as urbanization increases vulnerability
Earthquake Prediction and Preparedness:
• Prediction remains challenging; focus on forecasting probabilities
• Preparedness measures:
- Building codes for earthquake-resistant construction
- Early warning systems (seconds to minutes advance notice)
- Public education on 'Drop, Cover, Hold On'
- Emergency response planning and drills
- Securing heavy furniture and hazards in homes

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Earthquake causes effects regions plate tectonics geography SSS3 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The most common cause of earthquakes is:
quiz.explanation: Over 90% of earthquakes result from stress release along tectonic plate boundaries as plates move and interact.

quiz.q: At convergent plate boundaries, earthquakes occur because:
opts: Plates collide, causing subduction or compression
quiz.explanation: Convergent boundaries involve plate collision, creating compressional stress that builds until released as earthquakes.

quiz.q: The Pacific Ring of Fire is significant because it:
opts: Contains ~80% of the world's largest earthquakes
quiz.explanation: The Ring of Fire follows plate boundaries around the Pacific Ocean, where frequent subduction causes major earthquakes and volcanoes.

quiz.q: Soil liquefaction during earthquakes occurs when:
opts: Saturated soil loses strength and behaves like a liquid
quiz.explanation: Liquefaction happens when shaking causes water-saturated, loose soil to temporarily lose strength, causing buildings to sink or tilt.

quiz.q: A secondary effect of earthquakes is:
quiz.explanation: Tsunamis are secondary effects triggered by underwater earthquakes, whereas ground shaking and fault rupture are primary effects.

quiz.q: Human activities that can induce earthquakes include:
opts: Reservoir construction, mining, and fluid injection
quiz.explanation: Human-induced seismicity can result from large dams adding crustal load, mining altering stress, or fluid injection changing pore pressure.

quiz.q: The 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake caused a tsunami that resulted in:
quiz.explanation: The 2004 Sumatra-Andaman earthquake (M9.1) generated a devastating tsunami affecting 14 countries, causing massive loss of life.

quiz.q: In Nigeria, earthquake risk is generally:
opts: Moderate to low but not zero
quiz.explanation: Nigeria experiences occasional minor earthquakes, particularly in the southwest and north, but is not in a major seismic zone.

quiz.q: Earthquake preparedness measures include:
opts: Earthquake-resistant building codes and public education
quiz.explanation: Effective preparedness combines engineering (building codes), technology (warning systems), and social measures (education, drills).

quiz.q: The 'Drop, Cover, and Hold On' procedure during earthquakes is designed to:
opts: Protect from falling objects and structural collapse
quiz.explanation: This procedure minimizes injury by getting under sturdy furniture, protecting the head/neck, and holding on until shaking stops.
